Name: PACBayesLatencyAdaptation
Title: PAC-Bayes Framework for Latency-Adaptive Inference in Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that a PAC-Bayes framework can be adapted to optimize neural network inference under strict latency constraints, ensuring robust performance and bounded overhead.
Related Work: While PAC-Bayes frameworks have been used to provide generalization guarantees in various settings, their application to latency-critical inference is largely unexplored. Recent works, such as 'PAC-Bayesian Reward-Certified Outcome Weighted Learning' (Yu Ishikawa et al., 2026) and 'Generalization Certificates for Adversarially Robust Bayesian Linear Regression' (Mahalakshmi Sabanayagam et al., 2025), focus on robustness and uncertainty in decision-making but do not address latency constraints. Our proposal aims to fill this gap by explicitly incorporating latency into the PAC-Bayes framework.
Abstract: This paper introduces a novel PAC-Bayes framework for latency-adaptive inference in neural networks. We propose a method to optimize neural network inference under strict latency constraints while ensuring robust performance and bounded overhead. By leveraging the PAC-Bayes framework, we derive a theoretical bound on the inference time and accuracy trade-off. Our approach involves a Jacobian-based layer-skipping mechanism that dynamically adjusts the inference path to meet latency requirements. We validate our method through extensive experiments on benchmark datasets, demonstrating significant improvements in latency without compromising accuracy. Our contributions include a theoretical foundation for latency-adaptive inference, a practical algorithm for dynamic layer-skipping, and empirical evidence of its effectiveness.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Latency-Accuracy Trade-off Analysis - We will measure the trade-off between inference latency and accuracy on various neural network architectures (e.g., ResNet, MobileNet) using different latency constraints. We will compare our PAC-Bayes-adapted layer-skipping mechanism against baseline models and other latency-aware methods.

Experiment 2: Jacobian-Based Layer-Skipping - We will implement a Jacobian-based layer-skipping mechanism and evaluate its impact on inference time and accuracy. We will use the Jacobian to determine which layers can be safely skipped without significantly affecting the output.

Experiment 3: Robustness Evaluation - We will assess the robustness of our method under structured perturbations and domain shifts. We will evaluate the model's performance on out-of-distribution data and under adversarial attacks to ensure its reliability in real-world scenarios.

Experiment 4: Scalability and Resource Efficiency - We will evaluate the scalability of our method across different hardware platforms (e.g., CPU, GPU, edge devices) and quantify the resource usage (e.g., memory, FLOPs). We will compare these metrics to ensure that our method is computationally efficient and suitable for deployment on resource-constrained devices.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The PAC-Bayes framework may not always provide tight bounds, which could limit the practical applicability of the derived latency-accuracy trade-offs.
The Jacobian-based layer-skipping mechanism may not generalize well to all neural network architectures, requiring further investigation and tuning.
The method's performance may degrade under extreme latency constraints, where the trade-off between latency and accuracy becomes more challenging to manage.
Evaluating robustness under adversarial attacks and out-of-distribution data may reveal limitations in the method's ability to maintain performance under these conditions.
